message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 10
Recent Green phase: Added moonstone:25 to BASE_PREMIUM and "moonstone" to COMPONENT_TYPES. The existing per-type component grouping (countByType) now handles runes and moonstones as separate groups automatically.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all 10 tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass. This was largely a data addition; if no structural refactoring is warranted, document why. Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 10 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the implementation.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Going through every name in the file:

- `Item`, `QuoteStep`, `Step`, `Scenario` — domain types, clear intent.
- `BASE_PREMIUM` — record of base premiums by type. Clear.
- `COMPONENT_TYPES` — set of types treated as components. Clear.
- `BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_PREMIUM` — block of exactly 3 priced at 60. Clear and documented.
- `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE`, `PROCESSING_FEE` — clear.
- `roundPremiumUp` — clearly reveals intent, documented.
- `componentBasePremium(count, perComponent)` — computes base premium for a group of alike components. Clear.
- `sum` — clear.
- `isComponent` / `isMainItem` — clear predicates.
- `countByType` — counts items grouped by type. Clear.
- `itemsBasePremium` — base premium for a list of items. Clear.
- `quotePremium` — computes a quoted premium. Clear.
- `runScenario` — entry point. Clear.

The Green phase added `moonstone:25` to `BASE_PREMIUM` and `"moonstone"` to `COMPONENT_TYPES`. This is purely data. No name's intent changed: `componentBasePremium`, `countByType`, and `COMPONENT_TYPES` were already generic over component types, so adding moonstone did not narrow or broaden any function's purpose. **No rename warranted.**

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

The Green phase touched the two top constants and the per-type grouping logic. The relevant change locus is `itemsBasePremium` plus the data constants. Let me measure the data-affected region — `BASE_PREMIUM`, `COMPONENT_TYPES`, and `itemsBasePremium`:

```
BASE_PREMIUM: 6 string keys (constants, 6) + 6 numeric literals (constants, 6) = 12
COMPONENT_TYPES: 2 string literals (constants, 2) + Set invocation (2) = 4
itemsBasePremium:
  - bindings: items, mainItems, componentCounts, mainItemsPremium,
    componentsPremium, item(×2), type, count = ~10
  - invocations: filter(isMainItem), countByType, filter(isComponent),
    sum, map, sum, Object.entries, map, componentBasePremium,
    BASE_PREMIUM[](×2) = ~12 → ×2 = 24
  - no conditionals/loops/assignments at this level (delegated)
```

The structure is already small and flat. The grouping logic (`countByType` + `componentBasePremium`) is shared across all component types — moonstone reused it with zero new branching.

## Step 3: Apply the Four Rules of Simple Design

**Rule 1 — Tests Pass:** All 10 green. ✅

**Rule 2 — Reveals Intent:** Names are accurate and the two comments explain the non-obvious rules (block pricing, float-noise stripping). Adding moonstone did not degrade any name. No clarity improvement available that wouldn't be churn.

**Rule 3 — No Duplication:** I looked specifically for duplication the data addition might have introduced. There is none. Crucially, `moonstone` and `rune` are *not* duplicated as special cases anywhere — they live only as data in `BASE_PREMIUM` and `COMPONENT_TYPES`. The per-type grouping in `itemsBasePremium`/`countByType` already handles arbitrary component types uniformly. This is the key reason the Green phase was a pure data addition: the abstraction was already in place.

**Rule 4 — Fewest Elements:** No element became unnecessary. `componentBasePremium` and `countByType` remain justified (each used once but encode a distinct, named rule that would otherwise inline as noise). No new abstraction is needed for moonstone.

## Step 4: Refactoring Decision

I attempted to find at least one improvement against all four rules and APP mass. Candidates considered and rejected:

- **Inline `componentBasePremium` / `countByType`** (Rule 4) — would *raise* mass (adds a conditional + loop + assignment inline) and hurt Rule 2. Rejected.
- **Merge `BASE_PREMIUM` rune/moonstone since both are 25** — would be a Rule 3 over-application that conflates two distinct domain concepts that happen to share a value today; hurts Rule 2 and couples unrelated data. Rejected.
- **Rename anything** — no name misrepresents current behavior. Rejected.

The Green phase was, as the prompt anticipated, a pure data addition that reused an already-general abstraction. There is no structural change that improves any of the four rules or lowers APP mass without sacrificing clarity (Rule 2), which trumps APP.

I made no code change because every concrete candidate either increased mass, reduced clarity, or introduced false coupling — and the rules forbid sacrificing clarity for mass. I confirmed tests remain green (no change made, all 10 still pass).

## Refactoring Report

```
🔄 Refactoring Complete:
Naming:      Evaluated all identifiers — none misrepresent current behavior;
             COMPONENT_TYPES / countByType / componentBasePremium were already
             generic over component types, so adding moonstone changed no intent.
Rule 1:      All 10 tests pass ✅
Rule 2:      No clarity improvement available without churn.
Rule 3:      No duplication — rune & moonstone exist only as data, not as
             special-cased logic; per-type grouping handles them uniformly.
Rule 4:      No element became redundant; no new abstraction needed.
APP Mass:    No change possible that lowers mass without hurting Rule 2.
Refactoring: None applied — documented why (pure data addition onto an
             already-general abstraction).
Tests:       All passing ✅
```

The Green phase added moonstone purely as data (`BASE_PREMIUM`, `COMPONENT_TYPES`) and the existing per-type grouping absorbed it with no new branching. No structural refactoring is warranted: every candidate change either increased APP mass, reduced clarity, or coupled distinct domain concepts. The fact that this was a clean data-only addition is itself evidence the prior abstraction (`countByType` + `componentBasePremium` over `COMPONENT_TYPES`) was correctly factored.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
